lessons.title: Properties and Chemistry of Transition Metals

content.body: Transition metals are in the d-block of the periodic table (Sc to Zn). They have distinctive properties due to their partially filled d-orbitals.

Key Properties of Transition Metals:
1. Variable oxidation states (e.g. Fe: +2, +3; Cu: +1, +2)
2. Form coloured compounds (d-d electron transitions)
3. Act as catalysts (change oxidation state, provide surface)
4. Form complex ions with ligands
5. Paramagnetic (unpaired d-electrons)

Complex ion: Central metal + ligands
[Cu(H₂O)₆]²⁺ = blue; [CuCl₄]²⁻ = yellow

content.title: Iron as a Catalyst
content.body:
Haber Process: N₂ + 3H₂ ⇌ 2NH₃
Iron catalyst (with promoters) is used because:
- N₂ and H₂ adsorb onto iron surface
- Fe provides alternative mechanism with lower Eₐ
- Fe can change oxidation state to accept/donate electrons

questions.question: Why do transition metal compounds show colour?
options: d-d electron transitions absorb visible light
questions.answer: d-d electron transitions absorb visible light
questions.explanation: Electrons jump between d-energy levels, absorbing visible light — the complementary colour is observed

questions.question: Iron can exist in which oxidation states?
questions.explanation: Iron commonly exists as Fe²⁺ (ferrous) and Fe³⁺ (ferric)

questions.question: A ligand is a molecule that:
options: Donates a lone pair to the central metal
questions.answer: Donates a lone pair to the central metal
questions.explanation: Ligands are Lewis bases — they donate electron pairs to form coordinate bonds with the metal

questions.question: Why are transition metals good catalysts?
options: They can change oxidation states and adsorb reactants
questions.answer: They can change oxidation states and adsorb reactants
questions.explanation: Variable oxidation states allow electrons to be transferred; surfaces adsorb and activate reactants

questions.question: Which transition metal is used in the Haber process?
questions.explanation: Iron (with aluminium oxide and potassium oxide promoters) is the catalyst for ammonia synthesis

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

questions.question: What is the formula for calculating moles?
questions.explanation: Number of moles = mass (g) ÷ molar mass (g/mol). This is the fundamental mole equation.

questions.question: Which gas is produced when zinc reacts with dilute sulphuric acid?
questions.explanation: Zn + H₂SO₄ → ZnSO₄ + H₂. Metals above hydrogen in the reactivity series displace hydrogen from dilute acids.

questions.question: What is a covalent bond?
options: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.answer: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.explanation: In a covalent bond, two atoms share one or more pairs of electrons. Common in non-metal compounds like H₂O, CO₂, CH₄.

questions.question: What does the rate of reaction measure?
options: How fast reactants are converted to products
The energy released in a reaction
questions.answer: How fast reactants are converted to products
questions.explanation: Rate of reaction = change in concentration (or amount) of reactant/product per unit time.

questions.question: What is the effect of a catalyst on a reaction?
questions.explanation: A catalyst increases reaction rate by providing an alternative pathway with lower activation energy. It is not consumed.

lessons.title: Properties and Reactions of Transition Metals

content.body: Transition metals are d-block elements (Groups 3-12) that have partially filled d orbitals. They have distinctive properties unlike s-block metals like sodium and magnesium.

content.title: Characteristic Properties
content.body:
Variable oxidation states: Fe²⁺/Fe³⁺, Cu⁺/Cu²⁺, Mn²⁺/Mn⁷⁺
Coloured ions: Cu²⁺ (blue), Fe³⁺ (yellow-brown), Fe²⁺ (pale green), Cr³⁺ (green), MnO₄⁻ (purple)
Catalytic activity: Fe (Haber process), Ni (hydrogenation), Pt (catalytic converters), MnO₂ (decomposition of H₂O₂)
Complex ion formation: [Cu(NH₃)₄]²⁺ (deep blue), [Fe(CN)₆]³⁻

Complex ions:
Ligands are molecules or ions that donate lone pairs to metal ions
Coordination number = number of ligand donor atoms
Common ligands: H₂O, NH₃, Cl⁻, CN⁻, EDTA⁴⁻

Colour results from d-d electron transitions when d orbitals split in presence of ligands

questions.question: Which property is characteristic of transition metals but NOT Group 1 metals?
questions.explanation: Transition metals have partially filled d orbitals, enabling multiple stable oxidation states. Group 1 metals only show +1.

questions.question: What colour are Cu²⁺ ions in aqueous solution?
questions.explanation: Hydrated copper(II) ions [Cu(H₂O)₄]²⁺ appear blue due to d-d electron transitions.

questions.question: Iron is used as a catalyst in the Haber process for making...
questions.explanation: The Haber process: N₂ + 3H₂ ⇌ 2NH₃. Iron catalyst with Al₂O₃ promoter.

questions.question: A ligand donates...
options: A lone pair of electrons to the metal
questions.answer: A lone pair of electrons to the metal
questions.explanation: Ligands are Lewis bases — they donate lone pairs to the central metal ion to form dative (coordinate) bonds.

questions.question: MnO₄⁻ (manganate(VII)) is which colour?
questions.explanation: Potassium permanganate KMnO₄ contains MnO₄⁻ ions which are deep purple/violet.

questions.question: [Cu(NH₃)₄]²⁺ complex forms when...
options: Excess concentrated NH₃ is added to Cu²⁺ solution
questions.answer: Excess concentrated NH₃ is added to Cu²⁺ solution
questions.explanation: Adding excess ammonia to Cu²⁺ solution forms the deep blue tetraamminecopper(II) complex [Cu(NH₃)₄]²⁺.

questions.question: The colour of transition metal complexes arises from...
options: d-d electron transitions in split d orbitals
questions.answer: d-d electron transitions in split d orbitals
questions.explanation: Ligands cause d orbital splitting. Electrons absorb visible light to jump between d levels — the complementary colour is seen.

questions.question: Which transition metal is used in catalytic converters?
questions.explanation: Platinum (and palladium/rhodium) catalyse oxidation of CO and hydrocarbons to CO₂ and H₂O in car exhausts.

questions.question: Transition metals generally have high melting points because...
options: Strong metallic bonding using d electrons
questions.answer: Strong metallic bonding using d electrons
questions.explanation: Transition metals have more electrons (including d electrons) available for metallic bonding, giving stronger, higher-melting metals.

questions.question: Iron can exist as Fe²⁺ and Fe³⁺ because...
options: Its 3d and 4s electrons can both be removed
It forms two types of salts
questions.answer: Its 3d and 4s electrons can both be removed
questions.explanation: Fe can lose 2 electrons (4s²) for Fe²⁺, or 2+1 (4s² and one 3d) for Fe³⁺. The similar energies of 3d and 4s allow this.

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

content.body:
Complex ions: transition metal + ligands
Ligand: molecule/ion donating electron pair to metal
Coordination number: number of ligand donor atoms

Common ligands:
H₂O, NH₃: neutral, monodentate
Cl⁻, CN⁻, OH⁻: anionic, monodentate
EDTA⁴⁻: hexadentate (6 donor atoms)
en (ethylenediamine): bidentate

Colour: d-electron transitions absorb visible light
E = hf = hc/λ (energy gap determines colour absorbed → complementary colour seen)

content.title: Complex Examples
content.body:
[Cu(H₂O)₆]²⁺: octahedral, blue
[Cu(NH₃)₄]²⁺: square planar, deep blue (ligand substitution)
[FeCl₄]⁻: tetrahedral, yellow
[Fe(H₂O)₆]³⁺: octahedral, pale purple
[Co(H₂O)₆]²⁺: octahedral, pink
[Co(NH₃)₆]³⁺: octahedral, yellow

Chelate effect: polydentate ligands form more stable complexes (entropy increase)

content.title: Isomerism in Complexes
content.body:
Geometric (cis-trans): square planar and octahedral complexes
cis-[Pt(NH₃)₂Cl₂]: cisplatin — anticancer drug
trans-[Pt(NH₃)₂Cl₂]: inactive

Optical isomerism: non-superimposable mirror images
Chiral complexes with bidentate ligands
[Co(en)₃]³⁺: Δ and Λ forms

questions.question: A ligand is
options: molecule/ion that donates electron pair to central metal ion
questions.answer: molecule/ion that donates electron pair to central metal ion
questions.explanation: Ligand: Lewis base (electron pair donor) bonding to metal in dative (coordinate) bond

questions.question: [Cu(H₂O)₆]²⁺ has coordination number
questions.explanation: 6 water ligands → coordination number 6 (hexaaquacopper(II) ion, octahedral)

questions.question: Transition metal complexes are coloured because
options: d-d electron transitions absorb specific wavelengths of visible light
questions.answer: d-d electron transitions absorb specific wavelengths of visible light
questions.explanation: d orbitals split in ligand field; electrons can be promoted between split levels, absorbing visible light → colour

questions.question: Strong field ligands (e.g. CN⁻) cause
options: smaller d-orbital splitting → lower energy absorption
larger d-orbital splitting → higher energy absorption
questions.answer: larger d-orbital splitting → higher energy absorption
questions.explanation: Strong field ligands (CN⁻, CO, NH₃) cause large crystal field splitting → absorb higher energy (blue/violet) → appear orange/yellow

questions.question: Chelate effect: polydentate ligands form more stable complexes than monodentate because
options: forming one complex releases many small molecules, increasing entropy (ΔS positive)
questions.answer: forming one complex releases many small molecules, increasing entropy (ΔS positive)
questions.explanation: Chelation: replacing 6H₂O with 3 bidentate ligands releases 6 molecules → large +ΔS → more stable (ΔG=ΔH-TΔS more negative)

questions.question: Cisplatin [cis-Pt(NH₃)₂Cl₂] is used as
options: anticancer drug (binds DNA cross-links in cancer cells)
questions.answer: anticancer drug (binds DNA cross-links in cancer cells)
questions.explanation: Cisplatin: cis arrangement allows it to form cross-links between adjacent guanine bases in DNA, blocking replication

questions.question: EDTA is hexadentate meaning
options: it has 6 donor atoms forming 6 bonds to metal
questions.answer: it has 6 donor atoms forming 6 bonds to metal
questions.explanation: EDTA⁴⁻: 2 N and 4 O donor atoms → 6 coordinate bonds to metal → very stable complex (chelation)

questions.question: Adding excess NH₃ to Cu(H₂O)₆²⁺ solution changes colour from blue to deep blue because
options: NH₃ displaces H₂O as ligand forming [Cu(NH₃)₄]²⁺ — stronger field, different absorption
questions.answer: NH₃ displaces H₂O as ligand forming [Cu(NH₃)₄]²⁺ — stronger field, different absorption
questions.explanation: Ligand substitution: H₂O replaced by stronger-field NH₃, changing d-orbital splitting → different absorbed wavelength → deeper blue

questions.question: Geometric isomers in square planar complexes: cis has
questions.explanation: Cis: same groups adjacent (90° apart). Trans: same groups opposite (180°). Different properties despite same formula

questions.question: Why are transition metals good catalysts?
options: They have variable oxidation states allowing electron transfer and forming intermediates
questions.answer: They have variable oxidation states allowing electron transfer and forming intermediates
questions.explanation: Variable oxidation states: can accept/donate electrons → catalytic cycles. Also: d orbitals adsorb reactants (heterogeneous catalysis)

questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

lessons.title: Oxidation States and Reactions of Transition Metals

content.body:
Common oxidation states:
Ti: +2,+3,+4
V: +2,+3,+4,+5
Cr: +2,+3,+6
Mn: +2,+3,+4,+7
Fe: +2,+3
Co: +2,+3
Ni: +2
Cu: +1,+2
Zn: +2

Redox in transition metals:
Fe²⁺/Fe³⁺: -e⁻ oxidation; +e⁻ reduction
MnO₄⁻ (Mn⁷⁺) is strong oxidant
K₂Cr₂O₇ (Cr⁶⁺) is strong oxidant

content.title: Testing Oxidation States
content.body:
Identify Fe²⁺ vs Fe³⁺:
- Fe²⁺+NaOH → green ppt Fe(OH)₂
- Fe³⁺+NaOH → rust-brown ppt Fe(OH)₃
- Fe²⁺+KMnO₄/H₂SO₄ → decolourises (Fe²⁺ oxidised to Fe³⁺, MnO₄⁻ reduced to Mn²⁺)

MnO₄⁻ titration (in acid): purple → colourless
5Fe²⁺ + MnO₄⁻ + 8H⁺ → 5Fe³⁺ + Mn²⁺ + 4H₂O

content.title: Disproportionation
content.body:
Some elements oxidise and reduce simultaneously:
Cu⁺ disproportionates in water: 2Cu⁺ → Cu + Cu²⁺
H₂O₂: 2H₂O₂ → 2H₂O + O₂ (both oxidation and reduction of O)

questions.question: Fe²⁺ gives which colour precipitate with NaOH?
questions.explanation: Fe(OH)₂ is green/dirty green. Fe(OH)₃ is rust-brown. Used to distinguish iron(II) from iron(III)

questions.question: KMnO₄ is purple in acid. When Fe²⁺ added, solution becomes
questions.explanation: MnO₄⁻(purple)+5Fe²⁺+8H⁺→Mn²⁺(colourless)+5Fe³⁺+4H₂O. Decolourisation = end point in titration

questions.question: Highest common oxidation state of Mn is
questions.explanation: Mn in KMnO₄ is +7 (permanganate: MnO₄⁻). Most powerful Mn oxidation state

questions.question: Cr₂O₇²⁻ (dichromate) oxidises primary alcohol in acidic solution. Colour change from
questions.explanation: Dichromate orange/yellow reduced to Cr³⁺ (green). Used in breathalyser test for ethanol

questions.question: Disproportionation means
options: one element is simultaneously oxidised AND reduced
questions.answer: one element is simultaneously oxidised AND reduced
questions.explanation: Disproportionation: 2Cu⁺→Cu⁰+Cu²⁺. Cu⁺ acts as both oxidant (→Cu⁰) and reductant (→Cu²⁺)

questions.question: Vanadium shows oxidation states +2 to +5. Starting from VO₂⁺ (yellow), reducing with Zn/H⁺ stepwise gives colours
questions.explanation: VO₂⁺(+5,yellow)→VO²⁺(+4,blue)→V³⁺(+3,green)→V²⁺(+2,violet). Classic vanadium reduction experiment

questions.question: Fe³⁺ is reduced to Fe²⁺ when reacted with
options: iodide ions I⁻ (I⁻ oxidised to I₂)
questions.answer: iodide ions I⁻ (I⁻ oxidised to I₂)
questions.explanation: Fe³⁺+I⁻→Fe²⁺+½I₂. Fe³⁺ is oxidising agent, I⁻ is reducing agent. I₂ gives blue-black with starch

questions.question: Why does Cu⁺ not exist stably in aqueous solution?
options: It disproportionates: 2Cu⁺→Cu+Cu²⁺ because Cu²⁺ has more favourable hydration energy
questions.answer: It disproportionates: 2Cu⁺→Cu+Cu²⁺ because Cu²⁺ has more favourable hydration energy
questions.explanation: Disproportionation thermodynamically favoured: hydration of Cu²⁺ (+2 charge) more exothermic than 2×Cu⁺

questions.question: Corrosion of iron (rusting) involves Fe being
options: oxidised from Fe⁰ to Fe²⁺/Fe³⁺ in presence of O₂ and H₂O
questions.answer: oxidised from Fe⁰ to Fe²⁺/Fe³⁺ in presence of O₂ and H₂O
questions.explanation: Rusting: Fe→Fe²⁺ (oxidation), O₂+2H₂O+4e⁻→4OH⁻ (reduction). Both O₂ and H₂O required

questions.question: Galvanising protects steel by coating with zinc. Mechanism is
options: zinc oxidises preferentially (sacrificial anode) because it is more reactive than iron
questions.answer: zinc oxidises preferentially (sacrificial anode) because it is more reactive than iron
questions.explanation: Zinc is more reactive (more negative E°) than iron. Zinc oxidises preferentially, protecting iron even if coating scratched

questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

content.body:
Complex ions:
Transition metal + ligands (electron donors)
Coordination number: number of bonds to metal
Common: 4 (tetrahedral/square planar) or 6 (octahedral)

Ligands: H₂O, NH₃, Cl⁻, CN⁻, EDTA
Monodentate: 1 bond, bidentate: 2 bonds
Chelate: ring formed by polydentate ligand

Colour from d-d transitions:
high energy ligands → large Δ → absorb low λ
low energy ligands → small Δ → absorb high λ
Spectrochemical series: I⁻<Br⁻<Cl⁻<F⁻<OH⁻<H₂O<NH₃<en<CN⁻

content.title: Common Complex Ions
content.body:
[Cu(H₂O)₆]²⁺: blue (absorbs red/orange light)
[Cu(NH₃)₄(H₂O)₂]²⁺: deep blue (stronger ligand field)
[Fe(H₂O)₆]³⁺: pale yellow/brown
[Fe(SCN)]²⁺: blood red (test for Fe³⁺)
[Co(H₂O)₆]²⁺: pink
[CoCl₄]²⁻: blue

content.title: Ligand Substitution
content.body:
[Cu(H₂O)₆]²⁺ + 4NH₃ → [Cu(NH₃)₄(H₂O)₂]²⁺ + 4H₂O
blue → deep blue (Cu²⁺ test)

[Fe(H₂O)₆]³⁺ + SCN⁻ → [Fe(SCN)(H₂O)₅]²⁺ + H₂O
pale yellow → blood red (Fe³⁺ test)

Chelation therapy: EDTA removes heavy metal ions from blood

questions.question: Ligands are
options: electron pair donors that coordinate to metal
questions.answer: electron pair donors that coordinate to metal
questions.explanation: Ligands: molecules/ions with lone pairs that donate to metal centre forming coordinate (dative) bonds

questions.question: Colour of transition metal complexes arises from
options: d-d electron transitions absorbing visible light
questions.answer: d-d electron transitions absorbing visible light
questions.explanation: d-orbitals split in crystal/ligand field; electrons absorb specific visible wavelengths to jump between split d levels

questions.question: [Cu(H₂O)₆]²⁺ is blue because
options: it absorbs orange/red light and transmits blue
questions.answer: it absorbs orange/red light and transmits blue
questions.explanation: Cu²⁺ complex absorbs complementary colour (orange, ~630nm) → transmits blue

questions.question: Adding excess NH₃ to blue [Cu(H₂O)₆]²⁺ produces
questions.explanation: NH₃ is stronger field ligand than H₂O → larger d-level splitting → absorbs different wavelength → deep/royal blue

questions.question: Blood red colour with SCN⁻ confirms presence of
questions.explanation: [Fe(SCN)]²⁺ is intensely blood-red — specific test for Fe³⁺ ions in solution

questions.question: Bidentate ligand forms how many bonds to metal?
questions.explanation: Bidentate (bi=two): donates 2 electron pairs to metal centre → chelate ring formed

questions.question: EDTA is used in chelation therapy because
options: it is a hexadentate ligand that forms very stable complexes with metal ions, removing them
questions.answer: it is a hexadentate ligand that forms very stable complexes with metal ions, removing them
questions.explanation: EDTA wraps around metal from 6 directions → extremely stable chelate → used to remove toxic metals (Pb, Hg) from blood

questions.question: Spectrochemical series: stronger field ligands
options: cause larger d-orbital splitting (larger Δ) → absorb higher energy (blue) light
questions.answer: cause larger d-orbital splitting (larger Δ) → absorb higher energy (blue) light
questions.explanation: Strong field ligands (CN⁻, NH₃): large Δ. Electrons absorb short wavelength (blue/UV) to jump gap → complex appears orange/red

questions.question: [Co(H₂O)₆]²⁺ is pink. [CoCl₄]²⁻ is blue. This is because
options: Cl⁻ is weaker field than H₂O → smaller Δ → different absorption
questions.answer: Cl⁻ is weaker field than H₂O → smaller Δ → different absorption
questions.explanation: Cl⁻ weaker field → smaller d splitting → absorbs different wavelength. Also geometry changes: octahedral vs tetrahedral

questions.question: Coordination number of [Fe(CN)₆]⁴⁻
questions.explanation: Six CN⁻ ligands → coordination number 6 (hexacyanoferrate(II), octahedral)

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

content.body:
Variable oxidation states (transition metals):
Fe: +2, +3   Cu: +1, +2   Mn: +2, +4, +7
Cr: +2, +3, +6   V: +2, +3, +4, +5

MnO₄⁻: Mn(+7) strong oxidising agent (purple)
Reduced to Mn²⁺ (colourless/pale pink) in acid
To MnO₂ in neutral conditions

Oxidation state changes detected by colour changes

content.title: Redox Reactions
content.body:
MnO₄⁻ + Fe²⁺ in acid:
MnO₄⁻ + 8H⁺ + 5e⁻ → Mn²⁺ + 4H₂O
Fe²⁺ → Fe³⁺ + e⁻ (×5)
MnO₄⁻ + 8H⁺ + 5Fe²⁺ → Mn²⁺ + 5Fe³⁺ + 4H₂O
Purple → colourless (endpoint in titration)

Cr₂O₇²⁻ + 14H⁺ + 6e⁻ → 2Cr³⁺ + 7H₂O
Orange → green

content.title: Catalysis
content.body:
Heterogeneous: catalyst different phase to reactants
Fe catalyst: Haber process
V₂O₅: Contact process
Ni: hydrogenation of alkenes
Pt/Pd: catalytic converters

Homogeneous: same phase
Mn²⁺ autocatalysis: initially added, but also product of MnO₄⁻/C₂O₄²⁻ reaction — reaction accelerates as Mn²⁺ produced

questions.question: Transition metals have variable oxidation states because
options: electrons can be removed from incompletely filled d and s orbitals at similar energies
questions.answer: electrons can be removed from incompletely filled d and s orbitals at similar energies
questions.explanation: d-electrons readily lost: 3d and 4s close in energy → multiple stable oxidation states possible

questions.question: KMnO₄ solution is purple because
options: Mn(+7) in MnO₄⁻ gives strong absorption in visible
questions.answer: Mn(+7) in MnO₄⁻ gives strong absorption in visible
questions.explanation: Permanganate ion MnO₄⁻: Mn in +7 state, intensely purple — absorbs green light strongly

questions.question: When MnO₄⁻ acts as oxidising agent in acid, Mn is reduced to
questions.explanation: MnO₄⁻ + 8H⁺ + 5e⁻ → Mn²⁺ + 4H₂O. Purple→almost colourless: used to find endpoint in redox titrations

questions.question: K₂Cr₂O₇ is orange. After reduction to Cr³⁺, colour is
questions.explanation: Cr(VI) orange → Cr(III) green. Used as test for reducing agents and in breathalyser (alcohol detected)

questions.question: Heterogeneous catalysis involves catalyst
questions.explanation: Heterogeneous: e.g. solid Fe catalyst for gaseous Haber process reactants. Reaction occurs at catalyst surface

questions.question: Autocatalysis means
options: product of reaction acts as catalyst, so rate increases as product accumulates
questions.answer: product of reaction acts as catalyst, so rate increases as product accumulates
questions.explanation: Autocatalysis: product catalyses its own formation → sigmoidal rate curve (slow start, accelerates, then slows as reactants deplete)

questions.question: V₂O₅ catalyses 2SO₂+O₂→2SO₃ by
options: V⁵⁺ oxidises SO₂ to SO₃ (reduced to V⁴⁺), then re-oxidised by O₂ — cycles between oxidation states
questions.answer: V⁵⁺ oxidises SO₂ to SO₃ (reduced to V⁴⁺), then re-oxidised by O₂ — cycles between oxidation states
questions.explanation: V₂O₅ works via redox: SO₂+V₂O₅→SO₃+V₂O₄, then V₂O₄+½O₂→V₂O₅. Variable oxidation states enable this catalytic cycle

questions.question: Transition metals make good catalysts because
options: variable oxidation states allow redox cycles; d-orbitals adsorb reactants
questions.answer: variable oxidation states allow redox cycles; d-orbitals adsorb reactants
questions.explanation: Two mechanisms: (1) variable oxidation state cycles (homogeneous/redox catalysis); (2) surface adsorption/activation of reactants

questions.question: Oxidation state of Mn in MnO₄⁻ (charge -1, each O is -2)
Chromate(VI) CrO₄²⁻ is yellow; dichromate Cr₂O₇²⁻ is orange. Conversion: CrO₄²⁻ + 2H⁺ → Cr₂O₇²⁻ + H₂O means
options: adding acid converts yellow to orange
questions.answer: adding acid converts yellow to orange
questions.explanation: CrO₄²⁻/Cr₂O₇²⁻ equilibrium shifts with pH: acid → orange dichromate; base → yellow chromate

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.
